content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. “Nope” is my favorite alien movie right now.

**Score:** 55
**Body:** I’ve seen this movie like 6 times and it’s fun everytime 

I love the scene where Jean jacket uses their house to “defecate” all the things it sucked up and it’s raining blood.

Terrifying but hilarious 🤣

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's gonna be hard to get me off The Thing (1982) or Aliens (1986), but I really enjoyed Nope
  - I don’t think I seen The Thing yet. but also check out “The abyss”  it’s like a psychological ET movie.

Also there’s a movie called “sphere” with Samuel L Jackson …
    - John Carpenter was a HUGE influence for Jordan Peele. The Thing (1982) is a classic. I also really liked The Abyss. Sphere not as much.
      - That’s fair.
    - Holy FUCK! I cannot tell you how much I envy you right now.

Watch it this weekend. Lights off, phone away, volume UP.

Make sure it’s the OG version.
2. The abduction scene is one of the most horrific scenes in any alien movie. The realization of what’s happening inside the “ship”…
3. It had cool moments but there was a lot of unpacking and shit going on that felt a bit forced or extraneous and the whole “we must film it” thing was just kind of weird to me. Why?

I thought it was cool, but there are tons of better alien movies imm. I like JP a lot tho.
4. It’s done brilliantly and is one of my favourites, without a doubt. 😀
5. About a flying bed sheet?
6. Nope is legit one of my favourite films of all time. No one gets why but everything just works for me. It's scary, funny, the brother/sister relationship, the monster is genuinely terrifying and is scarier after the reveal, the whole monkey b plot...

Love it

## 2. Films on loneliness/existentialism.

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Desperately need a movie on deep harboured loneliness and isolation. I’d prefer female main character since i could relate on a more personal level but male would be fine too if the plot is meaningful. Like the kind where the character is always gloomy and is an existentialist. I low-key don’t want it to hv romantic interests; just pure solidarity suffering. 

Thanks. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lost in Translation definitely. And Nomadland.
2. Lost in translation
  - Groundhog Day
3. Little Miss Sunshine
4. Lost In Translation.
5. Castaway
6. *Moon* (2009)
7. Lost in Translation hits different when you're in that headspace. Scarlett Johansson's character is just floating through Tokyo feeling completely disconnected from everything, even her own marriage. No real romance, just two lonely people briefly connecting before going back to their isolated lives

  
Also check out The Virgin Suicides - it's haunting and melancholic in exactly the way you're describing
8. Oslo, August 31st 

Solaris 

You were never really here
9. Waking life

## 3. I'm looking for visually stunning Sci-Fi or Thriller movies. What have you watched recently that blew your mind?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I love movies like *Interstellar* or *Blade Runner 2049*. As a photography enthusiast, I really appreciate great cinematography. I’m open to any genre as long as it looks beautiful. Any hidden gems?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dark City
2. Prometheus and Alien Covenant were visually stunning. Some people didn't like them, I did.
3. Dune (2021) is absolutely gorgeous if you haven't seen it yet - Villeneuve knows how to make every frame look like a painting. Also check out Ex Machina, that movie is way more visually stunning than people give it credit for
4. If you are into photography, the original Tron is worth watching if you haven't seen it. The work they did with analog and film to get that look with minimal CGI is astounding.
5. The fall (2006)

It is the most visually stunning movie ever. It might be more drama but has some scifi elements. Check out the trailer at least.
6. Arcane. It has the most gorgeous animation I've ever seen.
7. Fifth Element, lol.
8. Annihilation
9. The Cell, the Dune movies, and one called The Fall that is not sci fi or thriller, more like fantasy.
10. The secret Life of Walter Mitty. 

Doesn't fit into either of your genres but visually stunning all the same.

## 4. Is there any movie that can make me feel motivated to face life?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I'm sorry for this weird title. I'm just looking for a movie that can make me feel encouraged to face challenges and bear life in general. You know, like using this movie as a tool for me to confront and face things. I don't know how to put it right but I hope you get me.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The secret life of Walter Mitty
2. The Pursuit of Happyness hits different when you're going through it - that scene where Will Smith is crying in the bathroom while his kid is sleeping gets me every time
3. Legally Blonde 


Dead Poets Society 



The Secret Life of Walter Mitty


Brittany Runs A Marathon 


On the Basis of Sex


Nyad


The Greatest Game Ever Played 


October Sky 
4. The Shawshank Redemption is a classic
5. Lady in the Water
6. The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel is a favorite of mine when I need some life motivation.
7. Little miss sunshine
8. Following
9. Waking life
10. Whisper of the Heart (1995)

Wendy (2020)

## 5. Best movie to stream during this winter apocalypse

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m in the region projected to get the most snow accumulation. My better half and I have very different movie tastes. So, any recommendations? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Honestly The Thing (1982) is perfect for this - you're already snowed in so might as well lean into the paranoia and cabin fever vibes. Plus it's got enough horror for one of you and solid practical effects that might win over whoever's not usually into the genre
2. The Shining lol
3. Misery
4. The Shining. Going crazy while trapped in a snow storm seems like a fun time!
5. Out Cold, Ski Patrol, Hot Tub Time Machine

Why is everyone recommending scary movies lol
6. Storm of the Century.  A Stephen King classic that seems appropriate, with one of his best villains.
7. Does it have to be a good movie? The Day After Tomorrow is about surviving a polar vortex or as Kent Brockman would call it a Class 3 Killstorm.
8. The Lion the Witch and the Wardrobe

The Ice Storm 

The Revenant 

Groundhog Day 

Fargo 

The Hateful 8 

Inside Llewyn Davis 

Balto 

Into the Wild 

The Grey 

Nosferatu 

Frankenstein 2025
9. Better Off Dead! Excellent ski movie!
10. Train dreams

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are some movies that were filmed but never released?

**Score:** 869
**Body:** Picture of Robert Downey Jr as a Mexican in Jamie Foxx’s unreleased movie All Star Weekend. Apparently cancel culture is the reason the movie was indefinitely shelve.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Batgirl
  - It sucks cause I would've loved to see Brendan Fraser as Firefly
2. I worked on a film called Hippie Hippie Shake, which starred Cillian Murphy and Sienna Miller. It was about London counterculture and Oz magazine, and the subsequent trial for obscenity. It was due to be released in 2010.
  - From IMDB: *After the box-office failure of Green Zone (2010), Working Title's parent company, Universal Pictures, wrote off this movie in an attempt to partially off-set that movie's loss of over one hundred million dollars. The insurers reportedly stipulated that the original camera negative be destroyed.*

Well, damn. Next to the two you mentioned, looks like it had an all round descent cast.
    - Haha I also worked on Green Zone. I hope I’m not the one that jinxed them both 😆
3. The Collaboration, a film version of the play about Andy Warhol and Jean-Michel Basquiat - starring Paul Bettany and Jeremy Pope reprising their roles from the original West End and Broadway productions - was due to be released in 2023. It’s been completed since then, but has gone totally silent for unknown reasons.
4. The Day the Clown Cried.
5. Kung Fury 2 is done, finished, but apparently there's some legal issues due to some dipshits.

It has freaking Arnold Schwarzenegger in it!
6. Gore. 

The gore Vidal biopic staring Kevin spacey was fully wrapped and cut when Kevin spacey got cancelled / exposed. The film was on my radar and I had been looking forward to it. Netflix owns it. Maybe they will release it when spacey dies lol
  - Not what I was thinking when I saw 'Gore'...

## 2. Equilibrium. An underrated action film (2002)

**Score:** 691
**Body:** Starring Christian Bale, Mary O’Brien, Taye Diggs, & William Fitchner 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A guilty vice this one.  Excellent B film elevated by Bales performance and the unique gun fu concept.  A fun watch and great addition to the sci fi genre.
  - Sean Bean dies 
    - Hey! Spoilers!

Wait... No... It would be more of a spoiler if you spoiled films in which he *doesn't* die.

When that guy dies for real, people will be like:
#HE, and he alone, died the way he lived.
2. All I know about this movie was there were always a billion copies of it at any place that sold DVDs hah.
  - As the more popular movies kept being bought up and restocked, it kept the overall DVD inventory in Equilibrium.
3. I loved this movie when I was a teen; however, it always did irk me that these clerics are trained and drugging themselves to be emotionally hindered, yet Taye Diggs spends half the damn movie with that cheeky smile on his face.
  - I think that's sort-of the point. Kind of a "rules for thee but not for me" thing.
  - I thought that was hilarious the first time I saw it. Tae Diggs is a surprisingly likeable villain in Equilibrium.
4. The concept was fun, the action was unique and fresh, but Diggs and Bale made this movie great.
5. No...not without incident. 

This movie holds a personal place for me in my heart. I remember seeing it at the theater. It was only there for about 2 weeks and only had 2 showtimes. I was the only person in the theater and it was fucking awesome. Huge Christian bale fan. Wish he would do action movies again. 

The dark knight trilogy, harsh times, equilibrium, Terminator salvation, reign of fire. Those were the days of pure joy.

## 3. Quentin Tarantino’s list of actors he wanted for Pulp Fiction.

**Score:** 559

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting that Uma Thurman and Bruce Willis didn’t even make the list of possibilities.
  - And yet we cannot picture the top picks do as well.
    - I think Michael Madsen would have been equally good. But the oafishness that Travolta brought to the role was really special.
  - Tarentino has spoken about Willis getting the role. 

He never would've gone after him, considering Brice was one of the biggest stars in Hollywood and QT had only directed one move. But Willis got ahold of the script and reached out to him. He wanted to play Vince or Jules, but QT was set on Travolta at that point and wanted Jules to be black, so he sold him on Butch.
2. Fishburne & Madsen would have been a killer duo for Jules/Vincent
  - Laurence Fishburne, "Say what one more time. I dare you." would be excellent.
    - He wouldn’t yell. It would be quiet AF and we’d piss ourselves in fear
3. Eddie Murphy as Jules wouldve been either ground-breaking or career-ending move. Either that movie is gonna be a nightmare or a complete reinvention for Eddie. 


I really cant see it but maybe Tarantino saw something in Eddie that he put Eddie's name on the list.
4. “No rappers.”
5. Now I will die knowing the world will never get to see Christian Slater as Pumpkin

## 4. Favorite movie character who appeared in only a few scenes and had limited screen time, yet became iconic because of the performance. I'll start King Baldwin IV in kingdom of heaven

**Score:** 224

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 
Anthony Hopkins - Dr. Hannibal Lecter in The Silence of the Lambs (1991). Oscar for Best Actor in Leading Role with about 16 minutes on screen (24 minutes and 52 seconds all counted appearance).
2. Alec Baldwin in Glengarry Glen Ross
3. Capitain Koons (Christopher Walken) in “Pulp Fiction”
4. Th directors cut of that is absolutely amazing.  

But I’ll go with Viola Davis in Doubt.  One scene and it breaks your heart. 
5. Brando has two legendary ones.


Vito Corleone is only on-screen for 40 minutes out of almost 3 hours in The Godfather, and Colonel Kurtz is on-screen for just 15 minutes out of 2-and-a-half hours in Apocalypse Now.
  - Superman: his brief appearance as Jor-El made him the highest paid actor in history as he received millions fo 4 minutes screen time (undoubtedly surpassed now).
6. Ralph Fiennes in Schindler's List
7. Somehow Norton managed to have more emotional range in this role than the rest of the cast combined, and you never even see his face. Incredible performance.
8. Joe Turkel as Lloyd the bartender in The Shining.
9. Milton in Office Space.

## 5. Olivia Wilde Says Sex in Movies Hasn’t Been ‘Realistic’ for a ‘Long Time’ and Gen Z Doesn’t ‘Want to See Inauthenticity Anymore’ in Sex Scenes

**Score:** 222

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Now here's the twist, and there is a twist.. we show it. We show all of it.
  - Full penetration.
    - He smells crime
  - Because what’s the one major thing missing from all action movies these days guys? …*Full penetration*. Guys, we’re gonna show full penetration and we’re gonna show a lot of it!
2. Aren’t they the generation having the least sex of any?
  - Also the generation that likes to see it in films the least iirc
3. Ok. I will do it. I will bear this burden for the arts. I will have sex with Olivia Wilde for the sake of cinema!
  - Thank you for taking one for the team
4. Can you imagine? Hardcore sex in oscar nominated flicks? I mean, we have seen Olivia nude in Vinyl and everyone pretty much went silent about her after that. Or is it just me?
  - Anora and Poor Things, next question

I mean, not hardcore sex but quite graphic, both of them

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Flashbacks throughout Sinners

**Score:** 64
**Body:** I saw Sinners in theaters and just now again at home. I think it’s a good movie, not close to my favorite of the year. If I had to have one Coogler film I’ll take Fruitvale Station which I think is a 10/10, Sinners is like a 7/10 for me. 

There were a few elements that really broke immersion for me, like the constant format-switching and MBJ’s accent (I love him but sorry). I think the biggest issue by far, though, is the use of flashback. 

Three big examples jumped out on a rewatch:

1. An hour in, at the beginning of the dancers-across-time sequence, we get the voiceover explanation from the beginning of the film explaining the mythology again.

2. When Annie is dying, she mentions she has “someone waiting” and Smoke does, too, and we get a flashback to him placing the flowers on the grave. 

3. In the post-credit, when Sammie encounters Stack in the 90’s, we get another flashback voiceover of Annie explaining what vampires are and why they’re “the worst,” which explanation we got maybe 45 minutes prior. 

There is zero chance that an attentive audience would be confused in those moments w/o the flashbacks.

It just really feels like a choice made to cater to an inattentive home audience, the exact same “Netflix says we have to repeat the plot every few minutes” thing we keep hearing. 

If you pulled out the flashbacks *and even the initial explanations* I think the film would be much better. But those choices suggest that we, the audience, don’t remember what vampires are? Or why they’re here, which is repeatedly explained to us by multiple characters. 

Anyway, I think it’s a good film that could have been great if it wasn’t so clearly edited to suit the second-screen audience. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I just finished watching it and i definitely agree, ESPECIALLY the flashback with the flowers on the grave, it felt like they were treating the audience like children that can't retain any information and have zero attention span 

Reminded me a bit of how Netflix original films will be made with the assumption that people will be on their phone while watching, so the characters will reiterate the plot every few minutes

Probably a result of studio meddling but still, definitely took me out of the film
  - Seriously, I was shocked by how dumb the movie thought I was. I can't believe it got a best screenplay nomination, maybe if they had a "easiest to follow movie when you're 7 years old category" it'd deserve the nom.
2. These moments also stood out to me, and I'm hoping I get the chance to talk to the editor about it. The way I see it, there are three options:

1. It was a genuine creative decision by Coogler

2. It was a typical executive note

3. It was something that revealed itself as necessary through test screenings.

I used to be annoyed by the aliens in Arrival outright explaining what was going on, but I saw Joe Walker give a talk, and he said that the dialogue of that scene went through a ton of revisions (since it's all subtitles) specifically because audiences were getting confused, and they worked hard to make sure the information was properly conveyed.

The point being, it may have just been somebody at WB not trusting audiences, but it very much could have been feedback from the audiences themselves.
3. I have been saying this since I saw Sinners! The writing feels weak and juvenile- it doesn’t trust the viewer, it doesn’t let you interpret or ponder what’s happening on your own. *That’s* what art should be. 
It’s a fun popcorn movie. It’s Jurassic Park. What’s wrong with that? I don’t know why people are doubling down that there’s some sort of genius to it. 
  - Regarding this movie not trusting the viewer, here’s a fun little anecdote: during the scene where Smoke sees his dead wife and child, some guy in the theater next to me whispered to his wife “Who are they?” and she replied “His dead wife and child,” and he goes “They’re still alive?” This guy was in his 40s or something and was completely unable to pick up on the very obvious fact that Smoke was having a vision of the afterlife. Even though we were watching the same movie, I wondered if due to the difference in perception, we’d actually been watching very different films lmao. With the current state of media literacy, I can’t blame filmmakers for being more on the nose—with that being said, Sinners was an okay experience for me. A hard 6/10
  - I think it's ridiculous that you're comparing this to Jurassic Park. As an action film Jurassic Park is a masterclass in blocking, camera movement, editing, etc. Sinners might have higher artistic aspirations, it might attempt to make certain philosophical or sociocultural points, but in the very vague realm of what is usually considered a 'popcorn movie' (a term which I hate, but I'll use it for the sake of the argument), it is no Jurassic Park.
  - The movie struggled to articulate itself and fully deliver on the principle metaphor that white people in the USA are inherently vampiric of black culture. The concept was interesting, but the execution was so poor I’ve seriously wondered if I watched a different movie than everyone else. It certainly doesn’t warrant 16 Oscar nominations imo.
4. There is a big reason why this is a major studio blockbuster and as much as I agree it is a lazier form of storytelling, the general audience should not be given so much credit. They are not the attentive audience that you yourself are. Studios offer these notes to filmmakers and demand coverage be shot and the editors have to reference those notes and deliver the product as adequate as they can based on the expectations from the people who put the money behind the picture. 

There is much about Sinners that makes it a work of art, but it is a populist work of art and this is the same filmmaker behind Black Panther and its sequel. He knows how to play the game with a studio. His wisdom includes knowing that he has to rely on these editing callbacks because as many people in the audience might be like you, this movie is also made for everyone who isn't you.
5. I agree with all of that. My honest take on it is that it's a pretty corny film that hides behind a lot of impactful, tough symbolism. It kind of works, it kind of doesn't. I'm not surprised by how the critics are handling it. It's easy to brand it genius but is it...?

1. That same melody looping for the first half hour killed me. By the time they reach the hidden trucks, I was painfully aware I was watching a movie instead of being immersed in it.
2. Structurally, super repetitive. The constant trips and characters stopping to explain their backstories instead of letting things play out gives it a weirdly stagey feel. Or like someone turns vampire, everyone reacts, the vampire leaves, and then it happens again and again. Even the KKK shootout at the end felt less like escalation and more like another reset. Almost a handful of big scenes stitched together rather than one cohesive story.
3. Great vampire metaphor, but constant lazy villain logic. I was super tuned out by the time they kill Jack's character.
4. Performance-wise, I did not connect with Michael B. Jordan or Hailee Steinfeld. Both felt overdone to me at times. Michael's twins felt too similar to each other. He's a movie star no doubt, but imo has a really limited range (perhaps I just have ptsd from his Fahrenheit 451 and Fantastic Four). I kept thinking Jonathan Majors would have been a much stronger fit here.

That said, almost everyone else was excellent, especially Miles Caton, Delroy Lindo, Jayme Lawson, Omar Miller, Jack O’Connell, and Wunmi Mosaku.

And to end on an even more positive note, I really liked the ending (post-credit one). Clever.
  - I would buck against the word "corny" and option that it is sincere. Sincerity is a lost art in these kinds of films. But Coogler is a sincere storyteller and always has been. The man feels and he wants us to feel as well.

## 2. Day Of The Locust (1975)

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Has anyone seen this film? I read the novel it's based on years ago, but never saw the film adaptation until a couple days ago. I think its a fantastic entry into a microgenre I would call "the dark side of Hollywood" a la Sunset Boulevard, Mulholland Drive, etc. The pacing is relatively slow, especially for today's audience, but it has this incredible mounting sense of dread culminating in an almost apocalyptic climax. I also like how it focuses on the lower-level figures of Hollywood, people who probably weren't ever going to achieve their dreams of stardom. Its a scathing indictment of Hollywood, the place itself as well as the film industry. ​You really don't hear about this film all that much, and I think that's a shame.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love this movie and i almost never see it discussed anywhere. That finale sure is something else. And you get Donald Sutherland playing a slow witted oaf character named Homer Simpson.
  - Something else is right! And yes, Sutherland was a major highlight. It made me sick how they all treated him, but that's the brilliance of it. The naive, the slow-witted will be mercilessly used and taken advantage of in the moral cesspool of Hollywood.
  - I saw the movie when I was about 16: Homer Simpson stomping on that child was *burned* into my retinas.  I still think of it as one of the most disturbing things I’ve seen in film.

I love the movie, I rewatched it about a year ago and it is still incredible.
2. It does some things better than the novel. For instance, the "Burning of Los Angeles" is not really addressed at all as it is in the movie. In the book it's an idea that goes nowhere. Also, the little kid (that Homer Simpson stomps on in the end) barely exists in the novel but is given a few scenes in the movie to establish him. The movie does an excellent job of not only portraying the story but the writing style as well.
  - I wish I had seen the film immediately after reading the book so that I'd have a better comparison.
3. I've seen it three times I think, maybe more. Brutal, bleak film. If you want more recent takes on Hollywood's spiritual corruption check out Cronenberg's outstanding Maps to the Stars and Lynch's Inland Empire. I also like Under the Silver Lake a lot, but it's not for everyone. Babylon and The Neon Demon aren't as good, but worth watching too.
4. I always thought that Karen Black was inexplicably miscast. It’s been a long time since I read the book and saw the film (I just found a few days ago that it’s available on Hoopla and it’s now in my list). In the novel, as I recall, Faye is more of a late teenager, or in her 20’s. Black was around 35 or 36 when they filmed it. I get that being in her mid-30’s makes her failed dreams more tragic, but my recollection is that her casting wasn’t true to the novel. As I said, it’s been 50 years, so I could be wrong. And, I’ve wanted to rewatch it for some time; I will soon.
5. I liked this movie a lot - but I can see why some would not.

It's interesting how William Atherton was a leading man (or at least a lead character) in movies from the early 70s, but never made the jump to true star vs. character actor as he aged.
6. I think it’s a bad movie, but it’s the kind of bad movie I have a lot of sympathy for. It’s clear the filmmakers love Nathaniel West’s novel—so much so that they’re unwilling to do the kind of violence to it that a good adaptation requires. They try to wring every possible cinematic import out of every memorable scene, often turning a sentence or two into a full minute of runtime. The result is an overwrought, turgid slog. It doesn’t really stand on its own as a film, but it also doesn’t really do justice to the brutal efficiency of West’s prose.

IMO, the Coen brothers come much closer to a cinematic translation of Nathaniel West’s sensibility, especially with *Barton Fink*. And you're definitely right to mention *Sunset Boulevard* and *Mulholland Drive*. I'm actually curious now to know whether Billy Wilder and David Lynch read the novel...
  - Yes, that's a good point. *BARTON FINK* is *DAY OF THE LOCUST* done very well. Pretty much every single theme but with the power and intensity of the book.

West had a quality of expressing human cruelty that maybe was not possible to put on the screen. You certainly see that in *Miss Lonelyhearts*. *Fink* covered the zone.

## 3. The 2006 comedy film "Accepted" works as a really good metaphor for a critique of modern education, despite its light hearted comedy format. (overthinking alert!)

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Okay, so hear me out! 

Its a cheesy, 2000s comedy film with silly elements but the film actually works really well as a serious, almost politically anarchistic critique of the modern educational systems in most countries

The rough plot is that ambitious, charismatic yet underachieving student Bartleby "B" Gaines fails to get into any universities after graduating high school. After pressure from his parents, out of desperation he creates a fake college called the South Harmon Institute of Technology or SHIT (yes its a joke in the film) with some equally desperate friends. The college accidently gets listed as a real college online and ends up with hundreds of students which B then has to manage, without getting caught. 

He basically comes up with a system where the students set their own classes and goals and end up bettering themselves by coming up with their own educational classes which he funds. 

Maybe accidently, the film also makes the SHIT students fairly diverse. There is a former stripper who wants to get an education, a guy with severe ADD who claims getting into the college was the only time his parents were proud of him, a weird military looking kid who's parents dump him at the college to get rid of him, an ex CIA guy who has brain damage. One of the main characters Glen is considered weird and dumb but eventually excels in cooking (possibly a metaphor for neurodivergent folk or autism) etc. B's friends also include people like Hands, who was bullied into being an American football player but really had more of an interest in art and sculpture. When given the chance, he becomes a brilliant artist. 

A (possible accidental) metaphor for how ridged school systems are. If a kid excelled in a thing (like sports) the school might force the kid to peruse that thing, even if it is against the wishes of the kid. 

The ending sequence is a legal fight for the college to be accredited as a genuine institute. It suddenly turns from silly teen comedy to a serious courtroom drama where B delivers an *incredibly* articulate speech about the pressures and ideas that traditional, elitist educational institutions place on students. He argues that eventually, it seems like a contest of who can get the fanciest qualification rather than who can actually better themselves the most, which defeats the point of the spirit of education. He also criticises hazing rituals and the tradition of humiliation of people who are different. Its actually fairly well written and sympathetic for a comedy film. In the end, his conclusion is that education needs to be diverse, in order to tailor to different students' needs. Both traditional academic institutions can exist whilst alternative education forms like his SHIT college are also important. 

So there you are! I have dramatically overthought a film that was probably made in 5 minutes in a lab in the year 2005 to compete with films in the "American Pie" era! 

Its a great film honestly! Justin Long is top notch in that film! So is Jonah Hill. Actually solid acting for that genre. 

Also Jaysus what a terrible poster Accepted has! Graphic Design is my Passion level stuff! 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Writer-Directors that are better at one role than the other.

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I am going to start with the person that inspired this thread idea of mine, even though he is probably far from the best example as he only a co-writer of his films... often listed last of the writers. I have been getting into Park Chan-Wook recently, and I consistently have found myself wowed by his visual style. However, I often felt as if the scripts didn't live up to the visuals/editing/cinematography. I didn't feel this way with all of his scripts, but I felt it often enough. No doubt translation plays a significant issue as well with lack of subtlety etc. etc. Now, I still think he is one of the better auteurs in the biz, but it made me wonder how he might benefit from collaborating with different writers.

Naturally, who could talk about writer-directors without mentioning Tarantino, Oldboy's chief proponent at Cannes. Most people would likely say he is a better writer than director, but I have come to question that. Two Tarantino scripts have been used by different directors, but these scripts were significantly revised from my understanding, so using them as reference for Tarantino's director vs writing skills may be unwise. Fincher's use of the Clif Booth script will be a more fascinating look into how a different director will interpret Tarantino's work, but that is still a year away. Many people praise Tarantino's dialogue, but criticize how much of his visuals are lifted from other films. While this may deduct points for originality in directing, I don't think it is a real issue if he can use it to great effect. Meanwhile, I tend to think his dialogue requires decent actors to truly wring the juice out of it, which is a directing skill. I think more people would tend to say that Tarantino is a better writer than director, and I don't necessarily disagree, but I think it is worth making an argument for the opposite. 

Anyway, I mainly wanted to bring up this idea to see if other people had long standing feelings on this subject regarding certain directors, and would be willing to share their point of view. So please come up with some of your own examples.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chris Nolan is the obvious example. He is objectively not a bad writer in my opinion, but he is a superior director. Even the Oscar-winning Oppenheimer is oftentimes heavy-handed. His dialogues are often unnatural.
  - Christopher Nolan’s worst trait as a writer is when he has one character explain to another character something that’s also being shown visually
  - He's not great at scintillating or subtle dialogue, but he excells at plot logistics and story structuring.
2. I think Kevin Smith is an obvious choice and I'm sure he would agree with me. Great writer but a very mediocre director. His visual style is at best unremarkable and he famously knows less about basic film craft elements like lenses than most people who post about movies on Reddit(which is what pissed Bruce Willis off about him so much).
3. Tony Gilroy is a much better writer than he is director. So is David Koepp. Charlie Kaufman is the obvious writer then turned director and while his directing is excellent his sensibilities as a writer is just remarkable. 

I think Tarantino is unique. As a writer he’s very attuned to what kind of writing appeals to Hollywood yet his style still seeps through the pages. He understands the technical craft of writing a good screenplay. His directing is exactly the same confluence of style and attention to craft. I think he’s equal in both talents.
4. Aaron Sorkin feels like the free space here especially because his zippy writing style isn’t really compatible with his bog-standard direction. A movie like Chicago 7 really needs more tempo or a sharper edit or something to make the Abbie Hoffman-Jerry Rubin dialogue funny. And we’ve seen other people make that work with Sorkin! I think Mollys Game works but the others could’ve been so much better directed by someone, anyone else.

Warren Beatty is a better director than he is a writer. He goes into every project he directs with a strong vision and a craziness to see it through: Reds is a massive achievement in integrating documentary and narrative, Dick Tracy is nothing if not committed to the vision of recreating a comic book. But Beatty will be the first one to tell you that Elaine May deserves most of the credit for the Reds script, and the scripts for Shampoo and especially Bullworth I think are thematically confused and awkward with dialogue. Bradley Cooper is too early in his behind the camera career to say but I think he’s heading down the same path. 

When working in English I feel pretty safe saying Bong Joon Ho is not a great writer. I’ve never felt that about his Korean films so I’m sure it’s more of a language barrier than a “talent” issue. Turns out writing nuance and dark jokes is hard in a second language.
5. Ryan Coogler. Better director than a writer. His visual language in storytelling is remarkable in Sinners. But writing characters and dialogue, not so much. I wish he would have worked with a writer. He could have pushed the story in a lot more directions than he did.
  - It's also very clear that he had great ideas for Sinners, and it could have had a lot more depth than it did if the script was tuned up more.
6. I think Billy Wilder was a better writer than director. In all likelihood it's probably just a reflection of his mentality; story took priority over everything. It wasn't that he was a bad director; far from it, he was just very functional in the way he approached it, and I get the sense he directed in order to play the story out exactly how he envisioned, rather than for grandiose personal flourishes.

Kurosawa I'll tentatively put forward as a far superior director than writer, but as so many of his scripts were collaborative efforts with multiple others, it's hard to pinpoint just how much of them (which I tend to dislike or find mediocre) were his doing. Regardless, the direction clearly trumps the quality of the script in his films imo
  - I completely disagree on Wilder in that I think he is both a great director and writer. Even in a film like The Apartment it look superb visually.

## 5. David Lynch's "Rabbits": My interpretation

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I just finished watching it. Quite slow but I believe the pacing is an important aspect of the film. Upon my first watch, this was the best interpretation I could come up with:

I believe it is about concealed grief within a dysfunctional family. One feature of the film that is certain is that it is a sort of caricature of a sitcom, and in extension a caricature of domestic life. All of the dialogue is hard to recall as I just finished watching it for the first time, but this source of unease which penetrates the film I feel occurs from a singular event.. due to mention of an event.. "I was at the harbor when it happened" Suzie states at one point. Throughout the entirety of the film, we see the elements of a sitcom broken down in a disjointed manner. I took note that every time the clapping track plays when a character comes in, it is not accompanied by the character but is rather off-time, and as the film progresses grows more and more desynchronized, which I personally interpreted as the daily rituals of domestic life growing old, repetitive, miserable.. Perhaps in the shadow of something larger. I believe that there are also dysfunctional elements between Jack and jane, whom I believe are couples. The position assumed throughout the film features Jane at the ironing board, Jack on the left cushion of the couch, and Suzie on the right. The center is never occupied, apart from incremental moments with Jane. However, it is more often that when Jane approaches the two family members, she instead stands behind them. leaning over. The detail which I think is important being: When she is near the front at the couch, her shadow casts two heads behind her, leading towards the apparition of four rabbits on the wall. I find this curious as, if the lighting were consistent, this dual-light source would result in six shadows in total, but the film crew took the effort to what seems put a dimly lit spotlight in Jane's direction. I think I am reading too much into it but I do believe there is something there. And there is the notion of the middle cushion being empty, representing the absence of the fourth member, creating a distance between Jack and Suzie, and there already being a distance between Jane across the entire room, absorbed in repetition.

Of course, there is the element of the red deity which I am still clueless about. I believe it could be an intense manifestation of something... indigestable? I do not believe I could put a certain emotion on it.. Intense grief? intense rage? I believe it is the manifestation of the tension causing dysfunction in the family. During the poetry sessions Jack and Jane(??) seem to have, I found the spinning... what I could only call matchstick head... hypnotizing and strangely terror-inducing. The describe smoke, and smoke comes from it, filling the room, though never enough for use to see it come down, which I believe is the creation of this viscous toxicity that is apparent. I would also like to take note that if one pays attention, the change between the coach at house A, where most of the film occurs, and house B, where the poetry is spoken, are different, with house B having one which is rounder and does not have uplifted cushions. This is what makes me doubt the woman who sings the poetry is actually Jane. We see that throughout the entire film she wears a robe with the front loose, but at house B, with a different house, she has a dress distinct from the robe, and from any other character. This is what makes me believe that this is a different character entirely, and Jack is leaving, as described, late and "past midnight", to visit this unnamed character, in which both of them perform these rituals, of which purpose I don't know. I believe that somehow the red deity is also relevant to this, as an incarnation of *something*. They describe a man in a green suit, and dogs crawling and barking... I believe I know what this is referring to. There is a painting I've seen, titled simply *Man in A Green Coat Holding A Hare With Four Hounds At His Feet,* which I advise you look up, which is what I believe they are directly referring to. In their broken dialogue, they are perhaps discussing this event occurring, though I believe it is non-literal, and this is simply symbolism Lynch derived from the painting, as he was very literate in art, though I do not think the painting itself plays any significance.

It is eternally raining. It is eternally night; there are no periods between nights as we hear them say the time is 11:15, then past midnight, and the 8 again... they are trapped in some miserable loop, perhaps. These rabbits are constantly petrified. Suzie will "find out one day" perhaps about Jack and this unnamed character, who perhaps had involvement prior to the unnamed incident, though her directly addressing him is perhaps a demonstration of her going through denial due to the occurrence of this awful event. The sitcom is broken. The laugh and clap tracks aren't even synced properly, there is no air of performance from any of the rabbits, they are toiling away in repetition, and are all suspect of each other, and afraid of their own actions.

I believe this was a family who was already dysfunctional to begin with, with many awful things occurring, and with many skeletons in their closet.. But they played the role of a sitcom. Something awful happened, perhaps with a family member, and the eternal rain occurs, and it is always night. The red deity is their confrontation with this tension. It is above them, yelling and screaming, terrifying, awfully apparent, angry, but they ignore it. The unnamed incident shattered all of their acts derived from ignoring the dysfunction. The repetition of domestic life, the script being shuffled, the audience. It is broken down, and hollow, leaving them with their reality: abject misery from the situation they are in.

I had fun thinking of this one. I will miss David Lynch terribly and I want your thoughts about it.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That one is really haunting isn't it, I like your analysis of the un-analysable :) I seems to me if you can't get a grip on what's really happening, the film is doing it's job. If I had to give the overall feeling of the film a name, I'd call it existential dread. I found that film oddly comforting when I was younger, extremely depressed, dealing with undiagnosed neurodivergence + complex trauma and feeling deeply unrelatable. (No violins please, I'm old now and in a better place)

That's the thing about trauma, it tends to put people at odds with the society around them. Arguably it's not the event itself that's the most damaging, but the way others react to victims afterwards, with minimisation, ignorance and exile. That's the point where trauma evades recovery and becomes pathological. The film appears to map well onto such a reading.

edit: I think I left out the most ironic aspect - victims, or survivors if you will, of trauma, are not operating from anything alien or unearthly, the parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous systems are universal to everybody. Like a cascade effect, people are avoiding the trauma because it's fundamentally the same traumatic reactions the survivors are having. Where you'd think there'd be mutual understanding, connection and warmth on the basis of shared humanity, there is instead absence. If anything, I think the subtext (or text? I'm not knowledgeable enough to deploy cinema terms) indicates the need for an enlightened approach, a confrontation with truth instead of avoidance of it.
